skill.name: chatkit-botbuilder
skill.description: Guide for building ChatKit chatbots with OpenAI Agents SDK, MCP tool wrappers, FastAPI backends, and Next.js frontends.
skill.summary: Build ChatKit chatbots with agent tools, backend integration, and user isolation patterns.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but most are markdown examples, SQLModel session.exec calls, or normal JWT algorithm examples rather than executable malware. The audit confirms high-risk guidance because the skill recommends storing bearer tokens in localStorage and includes a NEXT_PUBLIC_OPENAI_API_KEY example that could expose secrets in client bundles.

high_findings.title: Client-Side OpenAI API Key Exposure Guidance
high_findings.description: The frontend environment example uses NEXT_PUBLIC_OPENAI_API_KEY with an OpenAI secret placeholder. In Next.js, NEXT_PUBLIC variables are bundled for browser use, so following this guidance could expose a real API key to every client.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact public environment variable name and secret-key placeholder are present in the frontend configuration section. The summary repeats that the frontend may use NEXT_PUBLIC_OPENAI_API_KEY, which confirms this is recommended guidance rather than an isolated typo.

high_findings.title: Bearer Token Storage in Browser localStorage
high_findings.description: The skill repeatedly recommends reading and writing JWT bearer tokens in localStorage. Tokens in localStorage are accessible to injected browser JavaScript, increasing account takeover impact if the application has any XSS flaw.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple sections present localStorage token handling as the normal integration path, including login storage and performance advice. This is not direct exfiltration, but it is high-risk authentication guidance for production apps.

medium_findings.title: Exception Details Returned to Clients
medium_findings.description: Backend examples return raw exception strings to ChatKit clients and HTTP responses. This can leak stack details, configuration values, or backend implementation information in production deployments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly place str(e) into client-facing error messages. The surrounding code is documentation, but users may copy it into production backends.

medium_findings.title: Development Test Token Bypass Pattern
medium_findings.description: The middleware example accepts tokens that start with test-token- when the environment is development. This can be legitimate for local testing, but a misconfigured production environment would bypass normal JWT verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bypass is gated on a development environment check and is documented for testing. The risk depends on deployment configuration, so this is a moderate concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many markdown backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell execution. These files are documentation and do not contain executable Ruby backtick calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are headings, paths, or markdown formatting in static documentation. No runtime shell invocation path was found in the reviewed skill files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQLModel session.exec Reported as Python exec
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged session.exec(query).all() as Python code execution. In context, this is SQLModel query execution against a constructed ORM query, not eval-like execution of arbitrary code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The method is called on a database session and receives SQLModel select queries. The document even contrasts unsafe missing user filters with a corrected filtered query.

low_findings.title: JWT HS256 Examples Require Secret Strength Review
low_findings.description: Several JWT examples use HS256. HS256 is not inherently weak when paired with a strong secret, and the guide states JWT_SECRET should be 32 or more characters, but copied test examples use the literal string secret.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static weak-crypto signal is partly a false positive because HS256 can be acceptable. Confidence is lower because the test fixture uses a weak literal secret that users should not copy into production.

dangerous_patterns.title: NEXT_PUBLIC Secret Variable Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A frontend .env.local example places an OpenAI API key in a NEXT_PUBLIC variable, which would expose the value in browser-delivered JavaScript.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous variable name and OpenAI secret placeholder appear together in the frontend environment section.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Storage Bearer Token Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples store and retrieve bearer tokens with localStorage and sessionStorage before attaching them to Authorization headers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is shown as normal authentication plumbing in the main frontend guide and login flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Error Detail Disclosure Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Backend examples place raw exception messages into client-facing error responses.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both examples use str(e) directly in response objects that can reach clients.

content.user_title: Build ChatKit Chatbots with Backend Tools
content.value_statement: Teams need clear patterns for connecting ChatKit conversations to real application actions. This skill provides architecture guides for Agents SDK, MCP wrappers, FastAPI services, and Next.js widgets.
actual_capabilities: Explains a full ChatKit request flow from React frontend to FastAPI backend.
Shows how to wrap MCP tools so each call receives the current user identifier.
Provides examples for authenticated fetch calls and ChatKit widget configuration.
Documents SQLModel query patterns for per-user task isolation.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for ChatKit endpoints, threading, and dashboard refresh behavior.
Outlines deployment settings for frontend and backend ChatKit integrations.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not include runnable project files in the scanned package.
Some authentication examples require security hardening before production use.
The examples focus on task-management workflows and may need adaptation for other domains.
The guide assumes familiarity with FastAPI, Next.js, JWT authentication, and OpenAI tooling.

use_cases.title: Add ChatKit to a SaaS Dashboard
use_cases.description: Use the frontend and backend patterns to add an authenticated assistant that can read and update user-specific records.

use_cases.title: Design Agent Tool Wrappers
use_cases.description: Use the MCP wrapper guidance to pass request context into agent tools without exposing raw user identifiers to the model.

use_cases.title: Review Multi-User Isolation
use_cases.description: Use the isolation checklist to review middleware, tool, and database controls for cross-user data access risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic ChatKit Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-botbuilder skill to outline the minimum frontend and backend pieces for adding ChatKit to my app.

prompt_templates.title: Create Backend Architecture Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-botbuilder skill to turn the FastAPI ChatKit backend pattern into an implementation checklist for my project.

prompt_templates.title: Design MCP Tool Wrappers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-botbuilder skill to design MCP wrapper functions that inject the authenticated user ID into each tool call.

prompt_templates.title: Audit User Isolation Before Launch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chatkit-botbuilder skill to review my ChatKit architecture for cross-user data leaks, weak auth flows, and unsafe deployment settings.

output_examples.input: Plan the ChatKit backend for a task assistant.
output_examples.output: A concise architecture plan covering JWT middleware, request context, ChatKit server handling, wrapped task tools, and database filtering by user.

output_examples.input: Review my MCP tool design for user isolation.
output_examples.output: A review that checks whether every tool receives a trusted user identifier, whether raw tools are hidden from the agent, and whether database queries enforce ownership.

output_examples.input: Prepare a launch checklist for a ChatKit dashboard.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering authenticated fetch calls, backend routing, domain configuration, streaming responses, dashboard refresh behavior, and security hardening items.

best_practices: Keep OpenAI API keys only on trusted backend services, never in public frontend variables.
Use secure, HTTP-only cookie sessions or another hardened token strategy for production authentication.
Verify user isolation at middleware, tool wrapper, and database query layers before launch.
anti_patterns: Do not copy public frontend OpenAI key examples into production applications.
Do not expose raw exception details to ChatKit clients or browser users.
Do not rely on agent instructions alone to enforce user isolation.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design ChatKit chatbots that connect OpenAI agents to backend tools and application data.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete app?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, examples, and checklists that developers adapt to their own app.

faq.question: Which stacks does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Next.js frontends, FastAPI backends, OpenAI Agents SDK, MCP tools, JWT auth, and SQLModel examples.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use as written?
faq.answer: Not without review. Some authentication and environment variable examples need hardening before production deployment.

faq.question: How does it approach user isolation?
faq.answer: It uses middleware authentication, wrapper-based user ID injection, and database query filters to separate user data.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
